exemplar.title: An experiment to investigate that stereotype threat will impair maths performance in women.

detailedExemplarData.title: An experiment to investigate that stereotype threat will impair maths performance in women.

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and focused aim with direct relevance to educational and workplace bias reduction
Theory of stereotype threat accurately described and explicitly linked to the investigation
Independent and dependent variables are precisely stated and operationalized
Null and directional research hypotheses are correctly formulated
weaknesses: Explanation of relevance is somewhat brief and could deepen policy connections
Limited discussion of cognitive mechanisms underlying stereotype threat
Justification for choosing a one-tailed hypothesis is not fully articulated
Background sociocultural concepts are not explicitly tied to the investigation until later sections
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction is very strongâaim, theory/model connection, and operationalized variables are all clearly presentedâbut it lacks some depth in theoretical detail and justification for the directional hypothesis. These minor omissions warrant a high-mid mark of 4/6.

strengths: Independent-measures design is not only described but its choice is well explained
Controlled variables are listed in a detailed table with rationale and methods of control
Standardized instructions and debriefing strengthen procedural consistency
weaknesses: Sampling justification is limited, with little discussion of how sample diversity affects generalizability
Materials (math test) lack evidence of piloting or validation to establish content validity
Reliance on test difficulty alone to control ability could introduce baseline variability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section demonstrates clear design explanation and rigorous control, but the sampling rationale and materials validation are underdeveloped. This places it solidly in the mid-band, meriting 3/4.

strengths: Appropriate choice of Mann-Whitney U test for non-parametric data
Graphical presentation correctly addresses the hypothesis with clear labels
Findings are linked to hypothesis and original study in interpretation
weaknesses: Averaging two p-values shows fundamental misunderstanding of inferential reporting
Comparisons across four groups do not align with the stated two-group hypothesis
Graph omits error bars and sample sizes, limiting assessment of variability
Discussion of descriptive statistics oversimplifies reliability without confidence intervals
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the student applied both descriptive and inferential statistics and produced a correct graph, critical errors in statistical reporting and misaligned comparisons substantially undermine the analysis. The depth of flaws justifies a low-band mark of 2/6.

strengths: Findings are discussed with explicit reference to the original stereotype-threat theory
Strengths and limitations are clearly stated and directly relevant to the investigationâs validity
Proposed modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations and well justified
weaknesses: Discussion is relatively brief and does not explore alternative explanations for null results
Linkages of independent-measures design strengths to specific validity enhancements are not fully articulated
Impact of convenience sampling on external validity could be more critically examined
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation excels in identifying relevant strengths, limitations, and justified modifications, and it situates findings within theory. However, its brevity and limited critical depth warrant a mid-high mark of 4/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph with correctly labelled axes, units and titles depicts mean scores for each group, directly addressing the hypothesis.  Minor stylistic issues (omitting error bars) prevent full marks, but the presentation is otherwise correct, so a 5 in the 5â6 band is justified.

The student explicitly states that the null cannot be rejected, links this to the descriptive data, and contrasts findings with Spencer et al.  While the conclusions are concise, they are clearly tied to both data and hypothesis, meeting the 5â6 descriptor; limited depth leads to a score of 5.

Both IV and DV are clearly stated **and operationalised**: IV â whether participants are told âgirls did worseâ, DV â âpoints scored on the math testâ.  The null and one-tailed research hypotheses are presented correctly.  No omissions or ambiguities are evident, justifying the top of the 5â6 band (6).

The sampling technique (convenience) and participant characteristics (age, gender, multicultural class) are given, and some reasoning is offered (time constraints, need for separate gender groups).  The justification is briefer and less analytical than for the design strand, so a 3 within the 3â4 band is appropriate.

Stereotype-threat theory is described in everyday and technical terms, supported with citation of Spencer et al.  The write-up explicitly links that theory to the present investigation (âThis study tests the theory of stereotype threat as it looks at the difference â¦â).  The description is accurate, though not highly detailed about cognitive mechanisms, so band 5â6 fits; a mid-band mark of 5 best represents the quality.

Findings are described and connected to the original study and theory, noting that results do not support stereotype threat.  The discussion is accurate but relatively brief and does not explore alternative explanations extensively, fitting the upper end of the 3â4 band (score 4).

The student states a clear aim â to investigate whether stereotype threat impairs girlsâ maths performance â and goes on to explain why this is important for educational and workplace settings.  By explicitly linking the potential findings to reducing bias and inequality, the relevance is more than merely identified; it is **explained** in context.  Minor brevity in the explanation keeps it from exemplary depth, so a solid 5 within the 5â6 band is appropriate.

Multiple strengths and limitations are **explained** with direct relevance to validity (time limit, noise, sample size, control of variables).  The depth is good but not exhaustive, so a 5 within the highest band is reasonable.

A detailed table lists each controlled variable with both a âwhyâ and âhowâ, demonstrating an **explanation** of control strategy.  Materials are enumerated with purpose.  This meets the upper descriptor for 3â4; quality merits the top of that band (4).

Three concrete modifications (pre-screening for maths ability, quieter room, larger sample) are explicitly linked to the corresponding limitations and justified in terms of validity and generalisability.  The justification is clear though not deeply methodological, meriting a 5 in the 5-6 band.

The student not only describes the independent-measures design but **explains** why it was chosen (avoid demand characteristics, fatigue, simultaneous testing).  This rationale constitutes an explanation rather than a simple description, warranting the upper band 3â4; the explanation is sound but succinct, so a 4 is awarded.

Both descriptive (mean, SD) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U) statistics are applied and appropriate to non-parametric small-sample data.  However, the student reports two p-values and averages them, indicating misunderstanding, and comparisons across four groups rather than two may not align with the stated hypothesis.  These errors place the work in the 3â4 band; a 3 best reflects the noticeable inaccuracies.

annotations.comment: Standardized instructions strengthen procedural consistency and reduce researcher bias.
The title concisely states the investigation aim, providing immediate clarity on the research focus.
The background defines sociocultural psychology effectively but does not explicitly link these concepts to stereotype threat in your investigation.
The definition of stereotype threat is clear and concise; consider discussing alternative definitions or seminal sources to strengthen theoretical grounding.
Mentioning Spencer et al. is appropriate; deepen your critique by evaluating its methodological limits and applicability to teenagers.
The summary of Spencer et al. (1999) is concise and effectively frames your replication rationale.
The discussion of real-world implications establishes clear relevance; consider linking more specifically to educational policy.
The aim is clearly stated; consider operationalizing variables here for earlier clarity of your design.
Omitting a pre-survey is justified by sample size, but discuss how this may introduce confounds in measuring mathematical ability.
The independent variable is precisely operationalized as stereotype threat introduction, facilitating replication and clarity.
The dependent variable is concretely defined as test scores, ensuring measurable outcomes.
Both null and alternative hypotheses are correctly formulated, aligning with IV and DV and directional expectations.
Why was a one-tailed hypothesis chosen? Justify the directional test in light of theoretical predictions.
The rationale for using an independent-measures design is well explained, addressing demand characteristics and fatigue.
The convenience sample is described but lacks deeper justification on how diversity impacts the generalizability of your findings.
The materials list is clear; consider including piloting or validation details for the math test to demonstrate content validity.
The bar graph effectively shows group means but omits error bars, limiting assessment of statistical variability.
Expand on how mean differences and SD values translate into practical significance for your hypothesis.
Averaging p-values indicates a misunderstanding; report the correct p-value or U statistic rather than averaging two values.
Clarify why the Mann-Whitney U test was chosen over parametric alternatives by referencing your dataâs distribution assumptions.
Standardized debriefing demonstrates strong ethical practice and ensures participants leave informed.
The controls table is detailed and explains both why and how each variable was managed, showcasing careful planning.
Reliance on test difficulty alone to control mathematical ability may allow variability; consider pre-testing participants for baseline ability.
Ethical procedures including informed consent, anonymity, and debriefing are clearly articulated.
Consider detailing parental consent procedures since participants are minors to fully align with ethical standards.
Comparing your descriptive data with the original study adds valuable analytical perspective.
The descriptive statistics table is accurate but should include group sample sizes (n) to clarify variability comparisons.
The interpretation of higher SD as reduced reliability oversimplifies statistical reliability; consider discussing confidence intervals.
The questionnaire modification is sensible; outline how results will inform participant selection and analysis.
The strengths of independent-measures design are described but lack critical linkage to your specific experimental constraints.
Discussion of sample size and representativeness demonstrates strong alignment with evaluation criteria for generalizability.
Concluding remarks raise theoretical implications; consider exploring alternative explanations for your divergent results.
The conclusion ties directly to statistical results and provoques critical reflection on the validity of stereotype threat theory.
Strengths are described but would benefit from linking each to specific bias mitigation or validity enhancement.
Incorporate discussion of how each control measure addressed specific limitations to balance strengths and weaknesses.
The limitation regarding time pressure is clearly linked to stress effects, demonstrating thoughtful evaluation.
Reflecting on pre-screening as a limitation and suggesting its reduction shows coherent critical evaluation.
Your evaluation of convenience sampling notes efficiency but should discuss its impact on external validity explicitly.
